title: ‘Hit Man’ With Glen Powell and Richard Linklater!
format: A full review of Linklater's Hit Man, then Glenn Powell and Richard Linklater on making it. Opens on the unconfirmed Sam Mendes Beatles-biopic casting scoop.

segments.heading: The Beatles Biopic Casting Rumor
segments.note: an unconfirmed scoop, and a Sam Mendes report card

films.title: Empire of Light
films.note: Mendes's last film and, to both hosts, a disaster. Sean calls it 'the most COVID shit of all time,' so bad they never really discussed it on the podcast.
The big hit right before Empire of Light; Sean liked it a lot.

films.title: Spectre
films.note: 'That movie stinks.'

films.title: Skyfall
films.note: 'Fucking masterpiece. Absolute classic.'

films.title: American Beauty
films.note: Mendes's debut; Amanda's favorite of his '90s work.

films.title: Road to Perdition
films.note: Sean: 'a really good movie.'

films.title: Revolutionary Road
films.note: Sean stands for it: 'the rare revolutionary road guy.'

films.title: Jarhead
films.note: Amanda mostly remembers it as 2005-06 Jake Gyllenhaal being really hot rather than as an investigation of war films.

films.title: Away We Go
films.note: The midlife-crisis movie; Amanda saw it alone in a since-shuttered Brooklyn theater.

films.title: Hit Man
films.note: Linklater's twisty rom-com thriller, co-written and produced by Glenn Powell, who plays Gary Johnson, a New Orleans philosophy professor moonlighting as a fake hitman for the NOPD, luring people who want someone killed into recorded stings. Loosely based on Skip Hollandsworth's 2001 Texas Monthly story; the real Gary was an eccentric but committed no murders. The hosts track how it shifts from a character comedy (Powell cycling through a dozen disguised hitmen) into a steamy romance with Adria Arjona's Madison, then into noir once she kills her abusive husband using what Gary taught her. Sean's watched it three times; both single out the notes-app scene near the end, Gary coaching Madison through a police interrogation on his phone while both perform for the listening cops, as the movie scene of the year. Ends on a deadpan happily-ever-after in which the two murderers raise kids together. Bought by Netflix out of the festivals for $20 million.

films.title: Bernie
films.note: Linklater's other Texas-true-crime comedy, also drawn from a Skip Hollandsworth story, which the show put in his hall of fame the week before. Both prefer Hit Man; Sean argues Powell does more with the nice-guy-capable-of-murder register than Jack Black did, and that Hit Man plays its ending straighter.

films.title: The Nutty Professor
films.note: Sean's comparison for the first 30 minutes: Powell as Gary playing between six and twelve disguised hitman personas, like Eddie Murphy's multi-character turn.

films.title: American Psycho
films.note: One of Powell's hitman disguises is a dead-on, unspoken Patrick Bateman: the suit, the tie clip, the slicked-back hair, played straight with no acknowledgment. Sean's favorite of the bunch.

films.title: The Killer
films.note: Fincher's hitman movie, also a Netflix title, offered as the contrast: Fassbender's operative belongs to a fancy secret conglomerate, where Hit Man is feet-on-the-ground, blue-collar, ask-around-a-strip-club stuff.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.note: on the pandemic phone calls, the rehearsal method, and Everybody Wants Some!!
segments.guest: Glenn Powell and Richard Linklater on

films.title: Hit Man
films.note: Powell brought Linklater the Hollandsworth article about 20 years after Linklater first read it (Powell was in junior high). They broke the whole story over pandemic phone calls, never once in the same room, expanding a one-paragraph anecdote about a woman Gary spared into a body-swap identity romance. Production moved from Houston to New Orleans partly for Louisiana's rebates, partly because the city fit Gary's many personas. The late interrogation set piece, where every lie surfaces at once, got a disproportionate share of their rehearsal time.

films.title: Everybody Wants Some!!
films.note: Powell and Austin Amelio's earlier Linklater film, which the Ringer worships; a modest commercial performer now finding cult status. Powell calls the ranch-rehearsal shoot the definition of what making movies is about. Amelio, written into Hit Man with him in mind, nearly walks off with the movie as Jasper.

films.title: Fast Food Nation
films.note: Powell's first Linklater collaboration, cast as a teenager alongside Paul Dano. He broke his wrist before shooting and feared he'd be fired; Linklater kept the cast in, reasoning there's always a kid with a cast in high school.

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: Linklater's spiritual progenitor to Everybody Wants Some!!, invoked for the same studio-ish-film-with-an-indie-release trajectory.

films.title: The Fall Guy
films.note: Powell's most recent great watch, seen a couple of days earlier. (It also comes up in the opening as the reason the Academy is exploring a stunt Oscar.)

films.title: Singin' in the Rain
films.note: Linklater rewatched it on a plane.

films.title: Un Flic
films.note: Jean-Pierre Melville's last film, with Alain Delon, which Linklater finally caught in Paris. He's glad to see it, though he agrees it's no one's pick for Melville's best.

films.title: The Mother and the Whore
films.note: Jean Eustache's three-hour film, recently remastered and reissued on Criterion; Linklater has returned to it over the years and rewatched it feeling close to the French filmmakers while in Paris.
